questions.q: Which axiom is NOT part of Mallat's definition of an MRA on $L^2(\mathbb{R})$?
questions.explain: An MRA explicitly violates finite-dimensionality: $V_0$ already has the integer translates of $\phi$ as an orthonormal basis, so $\dim V_0=\aleph_0$. The other three are exactly Mallat's nesting, density-and-separation, and dyadic-dilation axioms.

questions.q: If $\phi$ is the scaling function of an MRA and $\psi$ the associated wavelet, what is $W_0=V_1\ominus V_0$?
questions.explain: By construction $\psi\in V_1$ is orthogonal to $V_0$, and $\{\psi(\cdot-k)\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ is an orthonormal basis of the orthogonal complement $W_0$. Iterating dilations gives $\{\psi_{j,k}\}$ as an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})=\bigoplus_j W_j$.

questions.q: Take $V_j=\{f\in L^2(\mathbb{R}): f$ is constant on every dyadic interval $[2^{-j}k,2^{-j}(k+1))\}$ (the Haar MRA). What is the integral $\int_{\mathbb{R}}\phi(x)\,dx$ for the canonical scaling function $\phi=\chi_{[0,1)}$?
questions.explain: $\phi$ is the indicator of a unit interval, so $\int\phi=1$. The normalisation $\int\phi=1$ is forced (up to sign) by $\sum_k\phi(\cdot-k)\equiv 1$ — a partition-of-unity condition any MRA scaling function satisfies.

questions.q: Compute the Haar wavelet coefficient $\langle f,\psi_{0,0}\rangle$ for $f(x)=x$ on $[0,1)$ and $0$ elsewhere, where $\psi_{0,0}=\psi=\chi_{[0,1/2)}-\chi_{[1/2,1)}$.
questions.explain: $\langle f,\psi\rangle=\int_0^{1/2}x\,dx-\int_{1/2}^1 x\,dx=\tfrac{1}{8}-\tfrac{3}{8}=-\tfrac{1}{4}$. The first half of the ramp averages to $1/4$, the second half to $3/4$; the Haar coefficient is half their difference times the interval width.

questions.q: Which property of $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_{j,k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ with $\psi$ the Haar wavelet is FALSE?
choices: It is an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$
questions.explain: The Haar wavelet is $\chi_{[0,1/2)}-\chi_{[1/2,1)}$ — a step function with two jump discontinuities. The other claims hold: it is the standard textbook example of a compactly supported orthonormal wavelet basis with one vanishing moment.

questions.q: How many vanishing moments does the Haar wavelet have?
questions.explain: $\int\psi(x)\,dx=\tfrac12-\tfrac12=0$, so the $0$th moment vanishes. But $\int x\psi(x)\,dx=\int_0^{1/2}x\,dx-\int_{1/2}^1 x\,dx=-1/4\ne 0$, so the $1$st moment does not. Exactly one vanishing moment — that's why Haar barely sees linear trends; Daubechies-$N$ wavelets are designed to kill the first $N$ moments.

questions.q: What does it mean for an orthonormal wavelet $\psi$ to have $N$ vanishing moments?
choices: $\psi$ is supported on an interval of length $N$
questions.explain: Vanishing-moment conditions are about $\int x^k\psi=0$ for $k<N$. Equivalently $\hat\psi(\xi)$ has a zero of order $N$ at $\xi=0$. They control how fast wavelet coefficients decay on smooth signals: if $f\in C^N$ near $2^{-j}k$, then $|\langle f,\psi_{j,k}\rangle|=O(2^{-j(N+1/2)})$.

questions.q: The Daubechies-$N$ wavelet $\psi_N$ is supported on which interval?
questions.explain: Daubechies's filter $h_n$ has $2N$ non-zero taps, $n=0,\ldots,2N-1$, so the scaling and wavelet functions have support $[0,2N-1]$. Daubechies-$1$ is the Haar wavelet (support $[0,1]$, one vanishing moment); higher $N$ trades support length for smoothness and more vanishing moments.

questions.q: Why does Daubechies require the QMF condition $|m_0(\xi)|^2+|m_0(\xi+1/2)|^2=1$ on the filter symbol $m_0(\xi)=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt 2}\sum_n h_n e^{-2\pi in\xi}$?
questions.explain: Orthonormality of integer translates of $\phi$ is equivalent (via Poisson summation) to $\sum_k|\hat\phi(\xi+k)|^2=1$, and propagating that through the refinement equation $\hat\phi(2\xi)=m_0(\xi)\hat\phi(\xi)$ produces the QMF identity. Compact support and vanishing moments are independent design knobs imposed on $h_n$ separately.

questions.q: Apply one step of the Haar DWT to the length-$4$ signal $(8,2,4,6)$. What is the first detail coefficient $d_1$ (the high-pass output at position $0$, computed as $(x_0-x_1)/\sqrt 2$)?
questions.explain: The Haar high-pass filter takes pairwise differences and rescales by $1/\sqrt 2$: $d_1=(8-2)/\sqrt 2=6/\sqrt 2=3\sqrt 2\approx 4.243$. The low-pass branch yields $(8+2)/\sqrt 2=5\sqrt 2$ at the same position; together they reconstruct $(8,2)$ via inverse rotation.

questions.q: What is the asymptotic cost of the Mallat pyramid DWT on a length-$n$ signal with a fixed filter of $L$ taps?
questions.explain: Each level convolves with a fixed $L$-tap filter and downsamples by $2$, so level $j$ costs $O(L\cdot n/2^j)$; summing the geometric series gives $O(Ln)=O(n)$. Strictly cheaper than the FFT's $O(n\log n)$ — and because each level halves the data, the whole transform is genuinely linear-time.

questions.q: In the standard pyramid algorithm, what does the inverse DWT do at each level?
choices: Upsample by $2$ then convolve with the dual filters and sum
Solve a linear system of size $n\times n$
questions.explain: Inversion mirrors analysis: the low-pass and high-pass coefficient streams are upsampled (zero-stuffed to twice their length), each is convolved with its dual reconstruction filter, and the two streams are added. For an orthonormal wavelet the dual filters are time-reversals of the analysis filters; the algorithm stays $O(n)$ overall.

questions.q: A wavelet atom $\psi_{j,k}$ occupies a Heisenberg cell of approximate dimensions
questions.explain: Dilation by $2^j$ shrinks support by $2^{-j}$ in space and stretches frequency content by $2^j$. The Heisenberg product $\Delta x\,\Delta\xi\sim 1$ — saturating the uncertainty principle — but the cell shape adapts: high-frequency atoms are short and tall, low-frequency atoms are long and narrow. Gabor windows, by contrast, force a fixed cell shape at every frequency.

questions.q: For which signal class do wavelet expansions outperform Fourier expansions most dramatically?
choices: Pure sinusoids on a long interval
Signals with isolated singularities or sharp edges
questions.explain: A jump discontinuity smears across all Fourier coefficients (Gibbs phenomenon, $|\hat f(\xi)|\sim 1/|\xi|$), but only the wavelets whose support touches the jump have large coefficients — a logarithmic number of them. Stationary or bandlimited signals are exactly where Fourier shines; wavelets give up phase coherence in exchange for spatial localisation.

questions.q: If $f\in C^N$ on a neighbourhood of $2^{-j}k$ and $\psi$ has $N$ vanishing moments, the wavelet coefficient satisfies
questions.explain: Taylor-expand $f$ to order $N$ at the centre and pair against $\psi_{j,k}$: the first $N$ terms vanish by the moment conditions, leaving a remainder bounded by $\|f^{(N)}\|_\infty\cdot 2^{-j(N+1/2)}$ (the extra $1/2$ is the $L^2$ normalisation $2^{j/2}$). This is the decay engine behind wavelet compression of smooth signals.

questions.q: Why does JPEG2000 use a wavelet transform instead of the blocked $8\times 8$ DCT of legacy JPEG?
choices: Wavelets compress better in the worst case for every image
Wavelets eliminate the blocking artefacts of fixed-tile DCT and degrade smoothly under aggressive quantisation
Wavelets are faster than the FFT-based DCT
Wavelets are easier to implement in hardware
questions.explain: JPEG's tiling produces visible $8\times 8$ blocks at low bitrates because each tile quantises independently. The CDF 9/7 biorthogonal wavelet in JPEG2000 is global and multi-scale, so high quantisation looks like overall smoothing rather than mosaic. Speed and worst-case ratio are not the differentiator.

questions.q: Donoho–Johnstone soft-thresholding denoises a signal by transforming, applying $\hat c=\operatorname{sgn}(c)(|c|-\lambda)_+$ to each wavelet coefficient $c$, and inverting. Why does this work?
choices: Wavelet coefficients of white noise are concentrated on a few atoms
Smooth signals have most wavelet coefficients near zero, while white noise spreads i.i.d. Gaussian mass over all coefficients — thresholding kills the noise floor
Soft-thresholding is the orthogonal projection onto the closest signal in $L^2$
Wavelet coefficients of white noise are exactly zero
questions.explain: The wavelet transform of white Gaussian noise is again white Gaussian (orthogonal change of basis). Smooth signals concentrate energy in few large coefficients; thresholding at $\lambda\sim\sigma\sqrt{2\log n}$ removes the noise floor while keeping signal spikes. Donoho–Johnstone proved this is asymptotically minimax over Besov balls.

questions.q: Why are wavelets a natural basis for Calderón–Zygmund operators (e.g. the Hilbert transform, $-\Delta^{-1}$)?
choices: Vanishing moments make $\langle T\psi_{j,k},\psi_{j',k'}\rangle$ decay rapidly off the diagonal $(j,k)=(j',k')$, compressing $T$ to a sparse matrix
CZ operators commute with translation and dilation
questions.explain: Beylkin–Coifman–Rokhlin showed that for a CZ operator $T$ and a wavelet with $N$ vanishing moments, $|\langle T\psi_{j,k},\psi_{j',k'}\rangle|\le C 2^{-(N+1)|j-j'|}(1+|2^j k-2^{j'}k'|)^{-N-1}$. Most entries are negligibly small, so $T$ is approximated by an $O(n)$-entry sparse matrix — wavelets do not diagonalise $T$ exactly, but they nearly do.
